title: EI at work & school
description: Often the thing that separates good from great — and you don't need a title.
summaryPoints: Stay composed under pressure — one steady person can steady a whole room
Take feedback as info, not an attack, and you'll grow faster than everyone
Lead by trust and listening, not a title — people follow whoever 'gets' them
Praise out loud and specific; correct quietly and in private
Own your mistakes openly — it builds credibility, not weakness

content.title: The thing that gets you picked
content.body:
Two people start the same job with the same grades. A year later one's been handed real responsibility and one hasn't. The difference usually isn't smarts.

It's the stuff this whole course is about: staying calm when things get tense, taking feedback without sulking, reading the room, working well with people who annoy you.

That's **emotional intelligence at work and school** — and employers rate it sky-high.
content.imageAlt: A sprout growing up a staircase of arrows

content.title: Manage up and across
content.body:
You don't just answer to a boss or teacher — you work with them, and with teammates.

The skill is figuring out what each person actually needs and communicating it before they have to chase you. Stuck on a deadline? Say so early. Confused by an assignment? Ask now, not the night before.

Reading what people need and getting ahead of it is half of looking 'great' at anything.

content.prompt: You need a title or some kind of authority before you can really lead.
content.explanation: Influence comes from trust, listening, and making people feel understood — not from a job title. You can lead from any seat in the room.

content.prompt: What's the most emotionally intelligent move?
content.context: You're confused about an assignment that's due in a week.
options: Stay quiet and figure it out alone the night before
Ask a clear question now, while there's still time to fix it
Wait and hope the teacher explains it again
Complain about it to your friends and do nothing
content.explanation: Communicating proactively shows you're on top of things. Asking early gives everyone time and beats a last-minute scramble.

content.title: Lead without a title
content.body:
You don't need to be in charge to lead. Real influence isn't bossing people around — it's the opposite.

People follow whoever makes them feel **understood**. Listen first. Make others feel heard. Be the one who notices when a teammate is drowning.

That's how a regular team member ends up being the person everyone actually trusts and turns to — no badge required.
content.imageAlt: Two heads connected by a bridge, representing perspective-taking

content.prompt: Match each situation to the emotionally intelligent response.
Does this build trust with people you work with, or quietly erode it?

items.text: Owning a mistake the moment you spot it
Listening before you jump in with your fix
Blaming a teammate when something goes wrong
Calling someone out in front of the whole group
Giving credit out loud when someone nails it
Going quiet and hiding a problem you caused

content.title: Praise loud, correct quiet
content.body:
Two simple rules make you the kind of person people want to work with:

- **Praise in public.** When someone does great work, say it out loud and be specific. 'You kept the whole group calm today' beats a vague 'good job.'
- **Correct in private.** If someone messed up, pull them aside. Never roast a person in front of the room — embarrassment kills trust fast.

Specific praise feels real. Quiet correction protects the relationship.

content.prompt: A teammate dropped the ball on their part of a group project. Best move?
options: Call them out in the group chat so everyone sees
Message them privately and ask what happened
Say nothing and silently redo all their work
Tell the teacher before talking to them at all
content.explanation: Correct in private. A quiet, curious check-in fixes the problem without the humiliation that makes people defensive.

content.title: Be the calm one
content.body:
Emotions are contagious. When one person panics, the panic spreads — that's **emotional contagion**. The good news: calm spreads the exact same way.

So when a deadline blows up and everyone's spiraling, the most valuable person in the room is the one who stays steady. One calm person can settle a whole team.

You don't have to fix everything. Sometimes just not adding to the fire is the win.
content.imageAlt: A thermometer cooling from hot to calm

content.instructions: One word flips this rule backwards. Tap it.
content.sentence: Owning your mistakes openly builds weakness with the people around you.
content.explanation: It's the opposite — owning a mistake openly builds credibility. People trust someone honest about slip-ups far more than someone who hides them.

content.prompt: Calm and panic both spread through a team the same way — this is called emotional ___.
content.hint: Like catching a mood instead of a cold.
content.explanation: Emotional contagion means feelings jump between people. That's why one steady person can steady the whole room.

content.prompt: You realize you made a mistake that affected your team. Put the moves in order.
steps: Notice the mistake and stay calm instead of panicking
Own it openly to the people it affected
Offer a fix or ask how to make it right
Adjust so it doesn't happen the same way again
content.explanation: Calm, then honest, then helpful. Hiding a mistake costs you trust; owning and fixing it earns it back.

content.prompt: Why does staying calm matter so much when a team is stressed?
options: Emotions are contagious, so your calm can steady everyone
It impresses the boss even if it changes nothing
It means you secretly don't care about the problem
Calm people always get the easiest tasks
content.explanation: Because of emotional contagion, your steadiness spreads. One composed person can pull a panicking group back to level.

content.title: Protect yourself from burnout
content.body:
EI isn't only about other people — it's also about noticing your limits. Running yourself into the ground doesn't make you tougher; it makes you worse at everything.

The skill is catching the early signs of overload and recovering **on purpose**: a real break, sleep, time off your phone, saying no when you're maxed out.

Great performers aren't the ones who never rest. They're the ones who recover before they crash.

content.title: First week
content.intro: New job, new class — fresh start. People are forming their first read on you. Earn their respect.

steps.situation: Your manager gives you tough feedback on your first assignment, in front of two others.

options.text: Listen, say thanks, and ask what to do differently
options.feedback: Taking feedback well is rare and noticed. You just looked coachable.

options.text: Get defensive and explain why it's not your fault
options.feedback: Defensiveness tells people feedback bounces off you — so they stop giving it.

options.text: Go quiet, nod, and quietly decide they're a jerk
options.feedback: Shutting down looks like sulking and you lose the lesson in the feedback.

steps.situation: A teammate is visibly stressed and falling behind right before a deadline.

options.text: Panic out loud about how you'll all miss the deadline
options.feedback: Your panic spreads — emotional contagion works both ways.

options.text: Stay calm and ask, 'What can I take off your plate?'
options.feedback: Calm is contagious, and offering help is how you lead without a title.

options.text: Ignore it; it's their part, not your problem
options.feedback: Heads-down indifference reads as 'not a team player' fast.

steps.situation: You realize a mistake YOU made is what set the team back.

options.text: Stay quiet and hope nobody traces it to you
options.feedback: Hiding it is the fastest way to lose trust when it surfaces — and it will.

options.text: Blame the unclear instructions you were given
options.feedback: Blame-shifting protects your ego and torches your credibility.

options.text: Own it right away and offer a fix
options.feedback: Owning a mistake openly builds credibility — it almost always reads as strength.

steps.situation: A teammate just pulled off a great save under pressure.

options.text: Praise it out loud and specifically, so the room hears
options.feedback: Specific public praise makes people feel seen — and it makes you the person they trust.

options.text: Say nothing so the spotlight stays off them and on you
options.feedback: Hogging credit is the quickest way to make a team stop rooting for you.

options.text: Mutter 'nice' without looking up from your screen
options.feedback: Half-hearted praise barely lands. Specific and out loud is what sticks.

content.outro: No title, no speeches — you stayed calm, took feedback, owned your slip, and lifted the room. That's how respect gets built.
